content.text: Why Conventional Commits Matter for Claude Application Repos

Claude application repositories mix several kinds of work in a single commit history: prompt engineering, tool schema definitions, agent orchestration code, evaluation suite updates, and infrastructure changes. Without a consistent commit convention, this blend of concerns makes it hard to answer two questions that matter most in production: what changed in the AI layer, and does this change require a breaking-change review cycle?

Conventional Commits enforce a machine-readable prefix on every commit message. For Claude repos, this prefix identifies not just the kind of change (feature, fix) but the AI-specific layer it touches (prompt, tool, agent, eval). That precision unlocks automated changelog generation, semantic version bumping, and CI rules that fire targeted checks on the right layer.

This sub-subtopic defines the Conventional Commits schema for Claude repos, the allowed type and scope values, the breaking-change protocol, how to enforce the convention with commit-msg hooks and CI checks, and how to read the resulting changelog to reason about AI model behaviour across releases.
Commit Message Anatomy

attrs.alt: Conventional Commit Message Anatomy for Claude Repos

content.text: Every commit in a Claude application repo follows this structure:

<type>(<scope>)[!]: <description>

[optional body]

[optional footers]
The components:
type
— the AI layer or change category (see table below).
scope
— the specific agent, model, or component affected. Omit only when the change is truly cross-cutting (e.g., a repo-wide dependency upgrade).

— appended to the type when the change is breaking: a prompt rewrite that changes output format, a tool schema field rename, or a removed capability. Breaking changes trigger a mandatory two-approval review gate and a full eval suite run in CI.
description
— imperative, present-tense summary under 72 characters. Describe the intent, not the diff. 'tighten refusal boundary' is better than 'update system_prompt.json'.
Type Values
The following types are defined for Claude application repos. Use exactly these strings — the commitlint config in the next section enforces them.
Type
Layer
When to Use
prompt
Prompt engineering
Any change to system prompt, user prompt template, or prompt assembly logic. Includes adding, removing, or reordering instructions.
tool
Tool definitions
Changes to function/tool JSON schemas, tool selection logic, or tool call parsing code.
agent
Agent orchestration
Changes to routing, multi-step planning, memory management, or agent-to-agent communication.
eval
Evaluation suite
New eval cases, updated golden outputs, changed scoring thresholds, or modified harness code.
fix
Bug / regression
A targeted fix for a specific misbehaviour confirmed by an eval case. Must reference the eval case or incident ID.
feat
New capability
A new end-to-end capability that spans multiple layers. Use sparingly — most AI-layer additions are better typed as prompt/tool/agent.
chore
Maintenance
Dependency updates, formatting, tooling config. No AI-layer changes. These should never trigger an eval run.
docs
Documentation
README, runbook, or inline comment updates only.
ci
CI/CD pipeline
Changes to workflow files, CI scripts, or deployment configuration.
Scope Values
The scope names the sub-system or agent affected. Teams define their scope list in the commitlint config so it is machine-readable and can be used to route CI checks.
Design rules for scope naming:
Use the agent's canonical identifier, not a human-readable label. If the agent is registered as `triage-agent` in the service config, that is the scope name.
For changes that touch the shared tool schema used by multiple agents, use `tool-schema`.
For evaluation suite infrastructure (harness, runner, reporting), use `eval-suite`.
For changes to the prompt hash registry or prompt version tracking, use `prompt-registry`.
Omit the scope (leave the parentheses empty) only for chore and ci commits that genuinely affect the whole repo.
Breaking Change Protocol
A breaking change in a Claude repo is any commit that makes an existing integration incompatible or causes a material change in output behaviour. Examples include:
A system prompt rewrite that changes the output format (e.g., switching from prose to structured JSON in responses).
Removing a tool from the available tool set that downstream consumers rely on.
Renaming or dropping a required field in a tool schema.
Changing the refusal threshold in a way that will reject inputs previously accepted — or accept inputs previously rejected.
To mark a commit as breaking:
Append ! immediately after the type (and scope if present): prompt(triage-agent)!:.
Add a BREAKING CHANGE: footer in the commit body that explains the incompatibility and what callers must update.
If the change introduces a new output format, include a migration example showing the before and after.

prompt(triage-agent)!: switch response format from prose to structured JSON

The triage agent now returns a JSON object instead of a prose summary.
This format is required by the downstream routing layer introduced in
agent(routing): add intent-based routing v2.

BREAKING CHANGE: Callers that parse the response as plain text will
receive a JSON string and must be updated to deserialise it.

Before:
  response = result["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]
  # response is "The user is asking about billing..."

After:
  import json
  payload = json.loads(result["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])
  intent = payload["intent"]       # "billing_inquiry"
  confidence = payload["confidence"]  # 0.91
  summary = payload["summary"]     # "The user is asking about billing..."

Eval ref: eval/triage-format-v2.json
Approved-by: @ai-safety-team
Enforcing the Convention
Commit-msg Hook
Install a local commit-msg hook so developers get immediate feedback before a commit lands. The hook runs commitlint against the staged message:

# Install commitlint and husky
npm install --save-dev @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventional husky

# Initialise husky
npx husky init

# Add the commit-msg hook
echo 'npx --no -- commitlint --edit "$1"' > .husky/commit-msg
chmod +x .husky/commit-msg

With this hook, a commit like `git commit -m 'update prompt'` is rejected immediately with a clear error listing what is wrong with the message. This catches malformed messages before they reach the remote, saving CI time and keeping the commit log clean.
CI Enforcement
The hook alone is not sufficient: developers can bypass it with `--no-verify` or push commits authored outside the local repo. Add a CI job that lints the commits in every pull request:

# .github/workflows/commitlint.yml
name: Commit lint

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0          # need full history to compare base..HEAD

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20

      - run: npm ci

      - name: Lint commits
        run: |
          npx commitlint                     --from ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }}                     --to   ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}                     --verbose
Routing CI Checks by Commit Type

Once commits carry reliable type metadata, CI can use that metadata to run targeted checks. A prompt commit should run the eval suite; a chore commit should not. The routing logic reads the commit range for the PR and decides which jobs are required:

# In CI, call the routing script and read the output
GATES=$(python scripts/ci_routing.py "$BASE_SHA" "$HEAD_SHA")
echo "Required gates: $GATES"

RUN_EVALS=$(echo "$GATES" | jq -r .run_eval_suite)
if [ "$RUN_EVALS" = "true" ]; then
  python -m pytest tests/evals/ -v
fi
Automated Changelog Generation
With consistent commit types, changelog generation is fully automated. The standard-version or semantic-release toolchain reads the commit history and emits a changelog grouped by type:

The resulting CHANGELOG.md is structured around the AI layers that changed, making it easy for on-call engineers to scan the changelog for prompt or tool changes when investigating a production incident:

## [2.4.0] - 2025-06-14

### Prompt Changes
- **triage-agent**: switch response format from prose to structured JSON (BREAKING)
- **summariser**: add length-control instruction for long-document inputs

### Tool Schema Changes
- **tool-schema**: add optional `source_url` field to citation tool

### Bug Fixes
- **triage-agent**: fix off-by-one in page-range extraction (#412)

### Evaluation Suite
- **eval-suite**: add 40 adversarial refusal cases for triage agent
Semantic Versioning Integration
Conventional Commits map directly to semantic version increments. In Claude repos, the mapping is:
Commit Type / Flag
Version Bump
Rationale
Any type with !
MAJOR (x.0.0)
Breaking change in AI behaviour or interface — callers must update.
feat
MINOR (x.y.0)
New capability without breaking existing integrations.
prompt, tool, agent (no !)
MINOR (x.y.0)
AI-layer change that affects output behaviour — treat as a feature, not a patch.
eval
PATCH (x.y.z)
Eval-only changes do not affect production behaviour.
fix
PATCH (x.y.z)
Targeted regression fix confirmed by evals.
chore, docs, ci
No bump
Infrastructure or documentation — no behaviour change.

This mapping is configured in the semantic-release commit-analyzer plugin. It ensures that a prompt change that is not breaking still produces a MINOR bump, signalling to downstream consumers that they should re-run their integration tests against the new version.
Testing the Commit Convention
Write unit tests for the commit parsing and gate-routing logic so that changes to the convention do not silently break CI routing:

# tests/test_ci_routing.py
"""Unit tests for commit type parsing and CI gate routing."""
import unittest
from unittest.mock import patch
from scripts.ci_routing import parse_type, compute_required_gates

class TestParseType(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_prompt_commit(self):
        ctype, breaking = parse_type("prompt(triage-agent): tighten refusal boundary")
        self.assertEqual(ctype, "prompt")
        self.assertFalse(breaking)

    def test_breaking_prompt_commit(self):
        ctype, breaking = parse_type("prompt(triage-agent)!: switch to JSON output")
        self.assertEqual(ctype, "prompt")
        self.assertTrue(breaking)

    def test_tool_with_scope(self):
        ctype, breaking = parse_type("tool(tool-schema): add source_url field")
        self.assertEqual(ctype, "tool")
        self.assertFalse(breaking)

    def test_chore_no_scope(self):
        ctype, breaking = parse_type("chore: bump commitlint to 19.x")
        self.assertEqual(ctype, "chore")
        self.assertFalse(breaking)

    def test_non_conventional_commit(self):
        ctype, breaking = parse_type("update the prompt")
        self.assertEqual(ctype, "unknown")
        self.assertFalse(breaking)

class TestComputeRequiredGates(unittest.TestCase):

    def _mock_commits(self, subjects):
        """Patch get_commits to return a fixed list of subjects."""
        return patch("scripts.ci_routing.get_commits", return_value=subjects)

    def test_prompt_commit_triggers_eval(self):
        subjects = ["prompt(triage-agent): update refusal instructions"]
        with self._mock_commits(subjects):
            gates = compute_required_gates("base", "head")
        self.assertTrue(gates["run_eval_suite"])
        self.assertFalse(gates["run_schema_compat"])
        self.assertFalse(gates["run_breaking_review"])

    def test_breaking_prompt_triggers_safety_review(self):
        subjects = ["prompt(triage-agent)!: switch response format to JSON"]
        with self._mock_commits(subjects):
            gates = compute_required_gates("base", "head")
        self.assertTrue(gates["run_eval_suite"])
        self.assertTrue(gates["run_breaking_review"])
        self.assertTrue(gates["require_safety_team"])

    def test_tool_commit_triggers_schema_compat(self):
        subjects = ["tool(tool-schema): add optional source_url field"]
        with self._mock_commits(subjects):
            gates = compute_required_gates("base", "head")
        self.assertTrue(gates["run_eval_suite"])
        self.assertTrue(gates["run_schema_compat"])

    def test_chore_triggers_no_gates(self):
        subjects = ["chore: upgrade Node to 22.x"]
        with self._mock_commits(subjects):
            gates = compute_required_gates("base", "head")
        self.assertFalse(gates["run_eval_suite"])
        self.assertFalse(gates["run_schema_compat"])
        self.assertFalse(gates["run_breaking_review"])

    def test_mixed_commits_union_of_gates(self):
        subjects = [
            "prompt(triage-agent): update refusal instructions",
            "chore: bump commitlint to 19.x",
            "tool(tool-schema)!: rename intent field to intent_type",
        ]
        with self._mock_commits(subjects):
            gates = compute_required_gates("base", "head")
        self.assertTrue(gates["run_eval_suite"])
        self.assertTrue(gates["run_schema_compat"])
        self.assertTrue(gates["run_breaking_review"])
        self.assertTrue(gates["require_safety_team"])

if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()
Good and Bad Commit Message Examples
These comparisons show the difference between a commit message that is useful for AI repo governance and one that is not:
Bad
Good
Why the Good Version Wins
update prompt
prompt(triage-agent): tighten refusal boundary for off-topic legal questions
Type + scope makes the layer and agent clear; description states the intent.
fix bug
fix(triage-agent): prevent empty-string intent when no tool call is returned
Describes the specific misbehaviour being corrected, not just 'bug'.
wip changes
eval(eval-suite): add 40 adversarial refusal cases for triage agent
Eval commits belong in the history so reviewers can see what coverage was added alongside a prompt change.
major prompt rewrite

prompt(summariser)!: replace extractive with abstractive summarisation strategy

BREAKING CHANGE: Output prose length now varies with input; callers that assert fixed-length output must update their assertions.
Breaking flag and footer make the incompatibility explicit and machine-readable.
cleanup
chore: remove unused eval fixtures from tests/evals/legacy/
Even cleanup commits benefit from the type prefix — CI skips the eval suite for chore commits.
Summary

Conventional Commits for Claude repos extend the standard convention with an AI-specific type vocabulary: prompt, tool, agent, eval. Each type carries precise semantics that CI can act on — routing the eval suite, schema compat checks, and safety-team reviews to exactly the commits that need them, rather than running everything on every change. The breaking-change protocol (! + BREAKING CHANGE footer) is the primary signal that downstream consumers must update their integrations. Automated changelog generation and semantic versioning are byproducts of the same discipline, making it possible to scan the release history and understand what changed in the AI layer without reading diffs.
